findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the first- and second-slice proving commands: either update the Binding implementation sequence's 'concrete proving command' sentence to match the two distinct commands specified in Binding comparative baseline (`--singleton --write-baseline` for slice 1; `--baseline` for slice 2), or update Binding comparative baseline to reflect the single unified command.
evidence: 'The concrete first- and second-slice proving command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing`' (Binding implementation sequence)

'The first-slice command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json`' (Binding comparative baseline)
'The second-slice command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --baseline src/...`' (Binding comparative baseline)
findings.finding: The 'Binding implementation sequence' section states 'The concrete first- and second-slice proving command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing`' (identical for both slices), while the 'Binding comparative baseline' section specifies a different first-slice command (`--prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline src/...`) and a different second-slice command (`--prerequisite-packing --baseline src/...`). An executor cannot determine which command to run as the gate for each slice.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence (step 1, final paragraph) vs. Binding comparative baseline (first and second paragraphs)
scenarios: Executor runs the sequence-section command for slice 1 (no --singleton --write-baseline), baseline is never written, second-slice comparison has no baseline to load and exits nonzero.
Executor follows the comparative-baseline commands and the sequence section's identical-command claim is silently violated, causing confusion in review.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Binding implementation sequence' step 1 final paragraph, replace the single-command sentence with: 'The first-slice proving command is `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json`; the second-slice proving command is `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json`.'  Remove the conflicting identical-command claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate the full `finalize_verdict` behavior under one section (or add an explicit cross-reference): specify both the verdict-string shortcircuit AND the array manipulation (move otherwise-blocking findings to `indeterminate` with `decision=indeterminate`/`reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, clear `blocking`) as co-located steps of the same function change.
evidence: Implementation: 'immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`, with existing branches changed to `elif`'

Binding comparative baseline: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, clear `blocking`, then return verdict `INDETERMINATE`'
No cross-reference between these two descriptions of finalize_verdict behavior
findings.finding: The Implementation section describes the `finalize_verdict` modification as only inserting `verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` (with `elif` rewrites for existing branches), but the 'Binding comparative baseline' section additionally requires moving every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate` and clearing `blocking`. These two descriptions of the same function's behavior are split across sections with no cross-reference, so an implementer following only the Implementation section will produce a verdict string change without the required array manipulation.
findings.location: Implementation section ('orchestrator.finalize_verdict' paragraph) vs. Binding comparative baseline section ('When prerequisite coverage is indeterminate...' paragraph)
scenarios: Implementer reads Implementation section, adds the shortcircuit, runs signal-chain tests (which only assert verdict string and `signed=false`), tests pass, but the verdict arrays are inconsistent — blocking array is non-empty while verdict is INDETERMINATE.
UI or downstream consumer reads `blocking` array when `verdict=INDETERMINATE` and sees findings that should have been moved to `indeterminate`.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section's `finalize_verdict` paragraph, append: 'Also move every finding in the `blocking` array into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, then clear `blocking`, before returning.' Add a back-reference note in the Binding comparative baseline section: 'See Implementation section for the complete finalize_verdict change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `PlanRelationSnapshot` is delivered and merged before this story begins, or define an explicit stub interface in this story's own file-impact list that the plan can build against without waiting for `75de`/`acaf` to land.
evidence: Plan: 'The pin interface supplies one review-time PlanRelationSnapshot preload keyed by authoritative canonical ticket ID'
Ticket 75de status: open; manifest lines: 'dep absent tests/unit/test_plan_material_pin_config.py', 'dep absent tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py', etc.
grep 'PlanRelationSnapshot' src/ → no matches in any .py file
Ticket acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c (the pin-emission story 75de depends on) also status: open
findings.finding: The plan asserts `PlanRelationSnapshot` is a 'certified material-pin interface from `75de-02b0-d454-410b`' that already supplies one review-time preload with 'relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and ticket plan text' — but ticket `75de-02b0-d454-410b` is `status: open`, every test file in its manifest is `dep absent`, and no symbol named `PlanRelationSnapshot` exists anywhere in `src/`. The entire prerequisite-routing mechanism (preload keying, focused finder routing, isolation assertions) rests on this undelivered contract.
findings.location: Upstream contract section; Implementation section ('Assert exactly one relation preload supplies both pin creation and prerequisite routing')
scenarios: Implementer attempts to write the prerequisite finder but has no PlanRelationSnapshot type, preload protocol, or field contract to code against — every isolation test and routing assertion is a forward reference to an unimplemented dependency.
AC 'One relation preload supplies both material-pin creation and prerequisite routing' cannot be tested or verified because the preload itself does not exist.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/relation_snapshot.py` (or equivalent) to this story's file_impact and define a minimal `PlanRelationSnapshot` stub (fields: `canonical_id`, `relation_kind`, `readable_state`, `pinned_material`, `plan_text`) so the prerequisite-finder can be implemented and tested independently of `75de`/`acaf`'s delivery.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `5967` must be merged before this story begins, or add `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` to this story's file_impact to extend it with `execution_review: bool = False` as part of this delivery.
evidence: Plan: 'pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls'
Ticket 5967 status: open; test files all dep absent
src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:678: def pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs) — no execution_review parameter
kernel decide.py:579: def pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs, *, threshold_for, impact_fn=None) — also no execution_review parameter
findings.finding: The plan states 'Determine `execution_review` once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by `5967` and pass the same boolean to both general and focused `pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review)` calls'. But ticket `5967-4476-7f9f-442d` is `status: open` (all its test files are `dep absent`), and the current `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs)` takes exactly two positional arguments with no `execution_review` parameter. This story cannot call `pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review)` without `5967` being merged first.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Determine execution_review once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by 5967 and pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls, applying the 0.8 execution floor consistently.'
scenarios: If this story ships before 5967, calling pass3_over_findings with execution_review raises TypeError.
If 5967 is silently assumed to land first, the delivery ordering dependency is invisible to the implementer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the blocking-to-indeterminate array movement happens in `plan_review_decide` (before `partition_findings`) or inside `finalize_verdict` (after partition), and ensure only ONE place is responsible; the current plan assigns the mutation to `finalize_verdict` but `finalize_verdict` only reads a pre-partitioned dict — resolve this architectural split explicitly.
evidence: Binding comparative baseline: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, clear `blocking`, then return verdict `INDETERMINATE`'

Implementation section: 'For the focused domain, missing, duplicate, malformed, out-of-range, `no`, or `na` attribution converts that prerequisite record to `indeterminate/attribution-invalid` and removes its incompatibility findings' — this happens in plan_review_decide
Current finalize_verdict code: reads pre-partitioned parts dict {'blocking', 'surfaced', 'overflow', 'indeterminate', 'dropped'} and never mutates it
Current partition_findings is the sole bucket-assignment function and runs inside plan_review_decide, before finalize_verdict
findings.finding: The plan specifies that blocking findings are moved into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` when `prerequisite_indeterminate` is true (Binding comparative baseline: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`'). However, `finalize_verdict` currently reads a *pre-partitioned* `parts` dict produced by `partition_findings` — blocking findings already sit in `parts['blocking']` before `finalize_verdict` runs. The plan also specifies that `plan_review_decide` converts attribution-invalid prerequisite records and 'removes its incompatibility findings'. These two descriptions create an ambiguous split: it is unclear whether the array mutation (moving findings to `indeterminate`) happens in `plan_review_decide` before `partition_findings`, or inside `finalize_verdict` after the partition. An implementer reading only the `finalize_verdict` modification section could change only the verdict string (adding the `if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` branch) without mutating `parts['blocking']`, producing a verdict of INDETERMINATE whose `blocking` array still contains findings — an inconsistent output that the Binding comparative baseline says must not happen.
findings.location: Implementation section ('This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`') and Binding comparative baseline ('move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array')
scenarios: Implementer adds prerequisite_indeterminate branch to finalize_verdict but does not move findings from blocking to indeterminate array, producing verdict='INDETERMINATE' with non-empty blocking array — inconsistent state passed to attestation sidecar and coach.

Implementer moves findings in plan_review_decide (pre-partition) but finalize_verdict still checks parts['blocking'] for non-empty — the prerequisite_indeterminate path never triggers because blocking is already empty when it is checked.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the blocking-finding movement to `plan_review_decide`: after both general and focused decide calls, if any focused record is `indeterminate`, re-classify all focused findings with `decision='block'` to `decision='indeterminate'` with `reason='prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate'` before the single `partition_findings` call. `finalize_verdict` then only needs to add the verdict-string branch (`if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate'): verdict='INDETERMINATE'`) without mutating `parts`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `PrerequisiteBlock` (dataclass or TypedDict) with at minimum `canonical_id: str`, `rendered_text: str`, and any other fields the finder prompt formatter and verifier context builder consume, co-located with or directly before the `pack_prerequisite_bins` definition in `sizing.py`.
evidence: Plan text: 'Define `pack_prerequisite_bins(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], *, subject_plan: str, system_prompt: str, model: str | None, per_block_output_tokens: int = PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS, headroom: float = DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM) -> tuple[list[list[PrerequisiteBlock]], list[PrerequisiteBlock]]`'
Plan text: 'Charge each whole block `est_tokens(block.rendered_text) + per_block_output_tokens`'

PrerequisiteCoverageRecord and PrerequisiteCoverageOutput are defined in the `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` section, but there is no corresponding definition of the input-side PrerequisiteBlock type anywhere in the plan.

The focused verifier also constructs per-finding blocks ('for each local finding, its authoritative prerequisite ID and plan block from the preload'), but the block's exact rendered shape is undefined.
findings.finding: The `PrerequisiteBlock` type used throughout the plan (in `pack_prerequisite_bins` signature, the finder user input construction, and focused verifier record assembly) is never defined. The plan defines `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` and `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput` but never defines a `PrerequisiteBlock` dataclass or TypedDict, even though it is referenced with a `.rendered_text` attribute in the `pack_prerequisite_bins` sizing section and implicitly in the finder's block-delimiter framing.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define `pack_prerequisite_bins`(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], ...)' and 'Binding implementation sequence step 4'
scenarios: An implementer building pack_prerequisite_bins does not know what fields PrerequisiteBlock carries beyond .rendered_text — e.g., whether it carries the canonical ID, a separate pin fingerprint field, or only text.
The finder prompt construction (PREREQUISITE id=<authoritative-id> blocks) relies on block structure not formalized in the plan, so the finder and packer could be built with incompatible assumptions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `sizing.py` (or a shared types module): `@dataclass(frozen=True) class PrerequisiteBlock: canonical_id: str; rendered_text: str` — and reference it from `pack_prerequisite_bins` and the finder/verifier call sites.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specification for `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_verifier.md` (prompt ID `plan-review-prerequisite-verifier`) to the 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts' section, including its system instructions, the attribution judgment rules (yes/no/never-na), and the input framing (subject plan + finding + prerequisite block).
evidence: Plan text: 'Add `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` and `PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER = "plan-review-prerequisite-verifier"` in `passes.py`'
Plan text: 'Create `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md`, resolved by prompt ID `plan-review-prerequisite-finder`.' — only the FINDER file is specified.

Plan text: 'The focused verifier receives the subject plan and, for each local finding, its authoritative prerequisite ID and plan block from the preload. It emits the existing `{index, severity_attributes, binary}` entry.' — no system prompt file or instructions for the verifier are specified.

All existing Pass-2 verifier prompts (plan-review-verifier, plan-review-verifier-agentic) have corresponding .md files in reviewers/. No analogous specification exists for the focused prerequisite verifier.
findings.finding: The focused Pass-2 verifier prompt is never named or specified. The plan names the finder prompt file (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md`, prompt ID `plan-review-prerequisite-finder`) and defines `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER`, but the focused verifier prompt — which receives 'the subject plan and, for each local finding, its authoritative prerequisite ID and plan block from the preload' — has no named file, no prompt ID constant (`PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER` is declared but only its name is given), and no instructions specification. The plan says 'The focused verifier receives the subject plan and, for each local finding...' but never specifies the system prompt content, file path, or prompt template.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` and `PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER = "plan-review-prerequisite-verifier"` in `passes.py`' and 'The focused verifier receives the subject plan and, for each local finding...'
scenarios: An implementer building the focused verifier call has no system prompt to load via `_resolve_system(PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER, ...)` — the prompt file does not exist and its content (what the verifier is instructed to do for attribution) is unspecified.

The attribution judgment ('yes only when the finding is supported by that exact pair, no for wrong or unprovable attribution') is the entire semantic purpose of the focused verifier, yet no prompt encodes it.

findings.finding: `PrerequisiteBlock` is named in the `pack_prerequisite_bins` signature (charging `est_tokens(block.rendered_text) + per_block_output_tokens` per block) and in the `_prerequisite_coverage_model` runtime, but its field structure is never defined anywhere in the plan. The field `rendered_text` is assumed but not declared; whether the struct also carries `canonical_id`, `relation_kind`, `pinned_material`, `ticket_plan_text`, or other upstream contract fields is unspecified.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 4: 'pack_prerequisite_bins(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], …) … Charge each whole block est_tokens(block.rendered_text) + per_block_output_tokens.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a definition such as: 'PrerequisiteBlock is a dataclass with fields canonical_id: str (the authoritative canonical ticket ID), rendered_text: str (the concatenated plan text from PlanRelationSnapshot, used for token estimation and prompt inclusion), and relation_kind: str.' Specify exactly how rendered_text is composed from the upstream snapshot fields.

findings.finding: The behavior of `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` when `prerequisite_indeterminate` is true — specifically, relocating every otherwise-blocking finding to the indeterminate array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate` — is specified only in 'Binding comparative baseline and compatibility behavior', not in the 'Implementation' section's description of the `finalize_verdict` change. An executor following only the Implementation section would implement the verdict-string flip and signing boundary but omit the finding relocation, producing an inconsistent response (verdict=INDETERMINATE with non-empty blocking array).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`'; Binding comparative baseline: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, clear blocking, then return verdict INDETERMINATE'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section's finalize_verdict paragraph, add after the verdict assignment: 'Also move every entry from the blocking list to the indeterminate list, setting decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, and clear blocking, so that verdict string and arrays remain consistent.' Then remove the duplicate description from Binding comparative baseline or add an explicit cross-reference.

findings.finding: The Implementation section's `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` modification says only 'add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"` … with existing branches changed to `elif`' — it says nothing about moving blocking findings to the indeterminate array or clearing the blocking array. The Binding comparative baseline section separately specifies: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the indeterminate array with decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, clear blocking, then return verdict INDETERMINATE.' An implementer following only the Implementation section will set the correct verdict string but leave blocking findings in the blocking array, contradicting the Binding comparative baseline behavior contract.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'This story explicitly modifies orchestrator.finalize_verdict'; Binding comparative baseline section — 'When prerequisite coverage is indeterminate, move every otherwise-blocking finding...'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section under 'This story explicitly modifies orchestrator.finalize_verdict', extend the description to read: 'add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): move every otherwise-blocking finding into the indeterminate array with decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, clear blocking, then set verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`, with existing branches changed to `elif`.' Remove the redundant restatement from Binding comparative baseline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Realign `plan_review_decide` to a single unified decide pipeline (one pass3_over_findings call, one reshape pass) consistent with the epic's 'No parallel decide pipeline is introduced' constraint, or update the parent epic first if the two-domain approach is now the approved design.
evidence: Epic (jira-reb-1160) Prerequisite consistency review section: 'Pass 3 preserves prerequisite_id on upheld findings, drops attribution=no candidates as unsupported, and maps missing/malformed verification to the unsigned INDETERMINATE coverage record. No parallel decide pipeline is introduced.'

Leaf plan: 'In plan_review_decide, independently reshape and decide each domain. Run the general path unchanged. For the focused domain, missing, duplicate, malformed, out-of-range, no, or na attribution converts that prerequisite record to indeterminate/attribution-invalid and removes its incompatibility findings.'
Leaf plan: 'Concatenate general_decided + focused_decided only after both decide calls return, then invoke the existing single partition_findings call with DET findings.'
Leaf plan: 'pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls'
findings.finding: The leaf introduces two independent decide sub-pipelines — 'independently reshape and decide each domain' with separate pass3_over_findings calls for general and focused arrays, and explicit concatenation of general_decided + focused_decided — which the parent epic explicitly prohibits: 'No parallel decide pipeline is introduced.'
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains… In plan_review_decide, independently reshape and decide each domain… Concatenate general_decided + focused_decided only after both decide calls return… pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(…) calls'
scenarios: A reviewer auditing the implementation finds two pass3_over_findings calls and separate index-domain reshaping in plan_review_decide, contradicting the epic's architectural constraint that no parallel decide pipeline exists.

The separate index domains and independent reshaping create a bifurcated decide stage that the epic explicitly ruled out, potentially introducing inconsistency between how general and prerequisite findings are scored.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the two-domain separate-decide architecture. Instead, fold prerequisite findings (with their prerequisite_id annotations and attribution fields) into the existing single decide pass alongside general findings. Attribution-invalid handling (converting a record to indeterminate) can be done as a pre-decide normalization step before a single unified pass3_over_findings call, preserving the epic's single-pipeline constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly whether `prerequisite_attribution_valid` is added inside the `Binary` sub-model (via the extra-binary-fields argument to `_build_binary` or `_build_verification_output`) or as a separate top-level field on `Verification`, so `code_review_verification_model` and `verification_model` remain byte-identical and only `plan_review_verification_model`'s Binary gains the new field.
evidence: Plan: 'Do not add the field to shared `_build_binary`, `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT`, or `GRADED_BINARY`'
Plan: 'its explicit `na` default and exclusion from `GRADED_BINARY` make it a plan-review-only veto rather than a validity contributor'
Current code: `_build_verification_output` calls `_build_binary(forbid)` → passes result as `binary` field of `Verification`; `_build_binary` constructs Binary from GRADED_BINARY constants
Plan: 'verification_model and code_review_verification_model pass no extra field and remain schema-identical'
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid`' but does not specify whether this extra field is added to the `Binary` sub-model (built by `_build_binary`) or directly to the `Verification` wrapper model. The current `_build_verification_output` calls `_build_binary(forbid)` to produce the Binary type, then passes it as a field of Verification. Adding a field to Binary vs. to Verification has different semantic and schema implications: Binary fields participate in the graded vocabulary enforcement; Verification-level fields do not. The plan says the field is 'a plan-review-only veto rather than a validity contributor' and is 'excluded from `GRADED_BINARY`' — which implies it should be in Binary (not in Verification), but the instruction 'Do not add the field to shared `_build_binary`' means a separate Binary-extension mechanism is needed. Without specifying where exactly the field lives in the output model, an implementer could place it at the wrong level, making `code_review_verification_model` and `verification_model` schema-identical to `plan_review_verification_model` in the wrong way.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument...'
scenarios: Implementer adds `prerequisite_attribution_valid` as a top-level field of Verification (not inside Binary), which means the verifier emits it outside the binary sub-object — prompt and output schema are inconsistent with how existing binary fields are named and accessed.

Implementer attempts to conditionally extend Binary inside `_build_verification_output` by passing extra fields, but the conditional logic for plan-review-only vs others creates a divergence in the Binary model type itself, subtly breaking the schema-identical invariant for the other two models.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit rules to the `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` schema section: (1) `disposition='finding'` with `findings=[]` maps to `output-invalid`; (2) `disposition='consistent'` with non-empty `findings` maps to `output-invalid` (contradictory record); include these as named schema-invalid cases in the 'Missing, duplicate, unknown-ID...' enumeration.
evidence: Plan text: '`finding` requires findings carrying that canonical `prerequisite_id` and no reason code.'
Plan text: 'Missing, duplicate, unknown-ID, parse-invalid, or schema-invalid output maps unresolved IDs in the bin to `output-invalid`'
The plan never states whether `disposition='finding'` with `findings=[]` is 'schema-invalid' (→ output-invalid) or structurally valid (Pydantic allows an empty list by default).
The plan also does not address the mirror case: `disposition='consistent'` with non-empty `findings` array.
findings.finding: The schema validation rule for `disposition='finding'` with an empty `findings` list is unspecified. The plan says '`finding` requires findings carrying that canonical `prerequisite_id`', but does not define whether a record with `disposition='finding'` and `findings=[]` is (a) schema-invalid → mapped to `output-invalid`, (b) silently coerced to `consistent`, or (c) a valid schema-conforming record that a downstream consumer must handle. This gap exists at the boundary between the `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` Pydantic validation and the `plan_review_decide` focused-domain reshaping.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Require exactly one record per readable pinned canonical ID. `consistent` requires no findings or reason code. `finding` requires findings carrying that canonical `prerequisite_id` and no reason code.'
scenarios: A model returns `{disposition: 'finding', findings: []}` for a prerequisite where it detected an issue but failed to populate the findings list. The reshaping logic does not know whether to treat this as output-invalid or as a spurious consistent.
A model returns `{disposition: 'consistent', findings: [{...}]}`. The decide step has no rule for which field wins.

findings.finding: The plan specifies adding optional prerequisite fields to `plan_review_decide_input.schema.json` and says 'extend decide output and coach input schemas with final coverage records,' but does not specify what field(s) `plan_review_decide` must emit in its OUTPUT dict, nor how the gate YAML (`plan-review.yaml`) wires that output to `plan_review_coach`'s inputs — which is where `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` is computed. Without an explicit output field name and YAML wiring, the prerequisite_indeterminate signal chain from decide→coach→finalize_verdict is under-specified for implementation.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'In plan_review_decide, independently reshape and decide each domain' and 'Wire focused finder and verifier steps and arrays through both branches in src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: '`plan_review_decide` adds `prerequisite_coverage: list[dict]` to its returned dict (alongside `blocking`, `surfaced`, etc.); the gate YAML passes `prerequisite_coverage: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.prerequisite_coverage }}` in the `plan_review_coach` `with:` block.'

findings.finding: The term 'tail conflict' is used in two normative, binding locations — 'tail-conflict fixtures' in the batching rule and 'two tail-conflict gold sets' in the fidelity spec — but is never defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer cannot write the fixtures or gold sets, and cannot know what scenario they are required to stress-test.
findings.location: Implementation section (batching rule): 'A tail conflict is evaluated identically to a singleton call'; fidelity spec: 'two tail-conflict gold sets'

findings.finding: The focused Pass-2 verifier batching strategy is described only as 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget' with no packing function, sizing algorithm, or named adapter analogous to `pack_prerequisite_bins` for the finder. An executor must invent the binning strategy (e.g., greedy token-count accumulation, fixed-count batching) without a specified algorithm, fixed-vs-variable cost model, or escalation path for oversized single-finding verifier inputs.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'The focused verifier receives the subject plan and, for each local finding...' and 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the counter placement and scope for `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` reason-code counters: e.g., whether they use the existing `record_contract_violation`/`drain_contract_violations` pattern in `orchestrator.py`, a new per-run counter dict, or a metrics registry call.
evidence: Plan text: 'Increment counters partitioned by reason code; never include prerequisite plan text.'
No counter module, class, or registry is named anywhere in the plan.

Existing observability in the codebase (e.g., `orchestrator.record_contract_violation` / `drain_contract_violations`) uses a module-level accumulator pattern, but the plan does not reference this pattern or specify an analogous mechanism for prerequisite counters.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Increment counters partitioned by reason code' for `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` observability events, but the placement, scope, and access mechanism for these counters is never defined. The plan gives no indication of whether these are module-level integers, a shared metrics registry, per-review state, or a thread-local accumulator — nor how callers read or reset them between reviews.
findings.location: Binding operational behavior: 'Emit structured event `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate`... Increment counters partitioned by reason code; never include prerequisite plan text.'
scenarios: An implementer writes module-level counters that accumulate across reviews in long-running processes, making the counters meaningless for per-review diagnosis.
A test asserting 'counters are partitioned by reason code' cannot locate the counter object without a specified placement.
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the existing `orchestrator.record_contract_violation` / `drain_contract_violations` pattern: use a module-level thread-local or run-scoped list, cleared per review via a `drain_prerequisite_counters()` call in the coach step.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC checkbox for observability, e.g. 'Every evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits a `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` structured event containing ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size (no prerequisite plan text); assertion proved by `pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py -k indeterminate_observability`.'
evidence: 'Add acceptance coverage for observability: tests assert each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate...' (Binding focused prompt and failure contracts)
ABSENCE: No AC checkbox in the Acceptance Criteria section covers structured event emission or counter partitioning.
findings.finding: The observability requirement ('tests assert each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size; counters are partitioned by reason; captured events contain no prerequisite plan text') is specified in 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts' with a dedicated pytest command, but has no corresponding checkbox in the Acceptance Criteria section. It is therefore not formally committed as a measurable deliverable and could be omitted without violating any AC.
findings.location: Binding focused prompt and failure contracts (observability paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: Executor completes all 14 AC checkboxes, CI passes, PR is merged, but the observability instrumentation is never implemented because no AC required it.
A reviewer approving against the AC checklist has no line item to check off for observability, leading to silent omission.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion covering the `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` observability event: it is emitted for every evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record with the required fields (ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, bin size), counters are partitioned by reason code, and no event contains prerequisite plan text.
evidence: Binding focused prompt: 'Emit structured event `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size for every evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid record. Increment counters partitioned by reason code; never include prerequisite plan text.'

Binding focused prompt: 'Add acceptance coverage for observability: tests assert each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate`...'
No AC in the Acceptance Criteria section references observability events, event fields, counter partitioning, or absence of plan text in events.
findings.finding: The structured observability event `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` — with its required fields (ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, bin size), counter partitioning, and prohibition on plan text — is a named deliverable with test-assertion coverage specified in 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts', but no Acceptance Criterion covers it. If the observability emission is omitted or incomplete, no AC will fail.
findings.location: Binding focused prompt and failure contracts section ('Add acceptance coverage for observability' paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: A developer omits counter partitioning or the bin-size field from emitted events — no AC catches it at review time.
An event accidentally includes prerequisite plan text — no AC enforces the prohibition.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'Each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid prerequisite record emits a `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` event containing ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size; counters are partitioned by reason code; no event contains prerequisite plan text.'

findings.finding: The `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` structured event (described in Binding operational behavior) is a concrete deliverable — it specifies exact fields, partitioned counters, and a privacy constraint — but no acceptance criterion covers its emission, field content, or counter behavior.
findings.location: Binding operational behavior section — 'Emit structured event plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate'; Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the key architectural decisions (two-domain finding isolation, prerequisite-scoped SHA-256 identity encoding, INDETERMINATE-before-blocking verdict ordering, optional attribution veto field design) in an ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design doc update referenced from this plan.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc anywhere in the plan
Multiple novel architectural decisions: two-domain index isolation, prerequisite-scoped identity encoding, optional attribution veto field, stable bin packing, two-slice empirical gating
'Reusing `plan_review_verification` is deliberate because the certified epic requires…' — this justification exists inline in the plan but is not captured in any durable doc artifact
findings.finding: The plan introduces a substantial new architectural pattern — a two-domain finding system, focused prerequisite LLM passes, new verdict routing, a fidelity evaluation framework, and a new contract layer — but specifies no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry to capture these decisions.
findings.location: Overall plan / Implementation section / Binding implementation sequence.
scenarios: A future developer modifying `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` or `plan_review_decide` does not understand the two-domain isolation invariant or why the prerequisite_indeterminate branch precedes blocking, leading to regression.
A reviewer adding a new verification contract copies `verification_model` instead of `plan_review_verification_model` and inadvertently omits the attribution veto field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a typed interface or named dataclass (e.g., `CoverageFlags`) for the `coverage` dict passed to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` so that `prerequisite_indeterminate` is an explicit, documented field rather than a stringly-typed key — and acknowledge the cross-component coupling in the plan.
evidence: 'build the existing local `coverage` dictionary with a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` key'
'pass that same dictionary through the existing `coverage=coverage` argument to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`'
No interface definition or typed contract for the coverage dict passed to finalize_verdict; it is a plain dict with an undocumented key namespace
findings.finding: The plan adds a direct structural coupling from `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` to the plan-review-specific key `prerequisite_indeterminate` in the `coverage` dict without defining an interface or event boundary to isolate the orchestrator from plan-review-specific semantics.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Wire focused finder and verifier steps…' and 'This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`'; `workflow_ops.plan_review_coach`.
scenarios: A second review type (code-review) later reuses `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` and its coverage dict accidentally includes a truthy `prerequisite_indeterminate` key from an unrelated context, forcing spurious INDETERMINATE verdicts.
A refactor of finalize_verdict does not know which keys in coverage are load-bearing, leading to silent deletion of the prerequisite_indeterminate check.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the plain dict with a `@dataclass CoverageFlags(prerequisite_indeterminate: bool = False, ...)` and update all call sites; this makes the coupling explicit, typed, and safe for future callers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete motivation section: cite at least one concrete example or evidence type (a sampled ticket review that passed but contradicted a prerequisite, a named gap in the existing general criteria, or a user/system problem statement) that demonstrates why the current plan-review gate is insufficient without focused prerequisite-consistency checking.
evidence: Goal: 'Evaluate whether a ticket plan remains consistent with each direct prerequisite without injecting prerequisite material into general reviewers or changing existing parent and child review behavior.' — describes capability, not a demonstrated gap.
No section in the plan names a concrete defect, a missed review, or a user/system problem caused by the current absence of this check.
ABSENCE: no Expected-vs-Actual, no failing test cited, no named incident or bug that motivates the change.
findings.finding: The plan adds a substantial cross-workflow mechanism (focused prerequisite-consistency criterion, new prompts, verdict wiring, observability, schema extensions) without demonstrating that the current review behavior is wrong or insufficient. There is no reproduction of a missed prerequisite-inconsistency, no named defect or user-system problem, and no Expected-vs-Actual showing the existing gate fails to catch prerequisite contradictions. The Goal section describes what the feature does ('evaluate whether a ticket plan remains consistent with each direct prerequisite') but not why the absence of this check has caused or risks harm.
findings.location: Goal section and Upstream contract section
scenarios: A reviewer asking 'why is this needed now?' has no evidence in the plan to evaluate whether the investment is warranted versus the risk of cross-workflow changes.
The extensive verdict-wiring changes (finalize_verdict modification, INDETERMINATE propagation) affect existing review paths; without a demonstrated need, the cost-benefit is unevaluable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Motivation / Current Behavior Gap' subsection before Implementation. Example: 'Current behavior: The Pass-1 general criteria do not receive prerequisite plan text (by design, per isolation contract), so a subject plan that contradicts an upstream interface commitment produces no finding. Observed in [example or sampled review]: [ticket X] passed review despite assuming interface Y from prerequisite Z that was removed/changed.' Even a single concrete illustrative case suffices.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the bespoke 4-byte-length-prefix binary encoding for prerequisite finding IDs is necessary over adding a domain-separated field to the existing pipe-delimited string encoding already used in `mint_finding_id`; if not, use `'prerequisite-finding-id-v1|' + finding + '|' + criteria + '|' + prerequisite_id` as the hash basis to stay consistent with the existing pattern.
evidence: Existing `mint_finding_id` in `orchestrator.py:332`: `basis = finding.get('finding', '') + '|' + ','.join(sorted(finding.get('criteria', [])))` then `hashlib.sha256(basis.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()[:16]` — pipe-delimited string hash already provides stable identity

Plan specifies: 'encode four fields as UTF-8, prefix each with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length, and concatenate them in this order: fixed version string `prerequisite-finding-id-v1`, finding text, comma-joined sorted criteria, and authoritative prerequisite ID. Hash the concatenated bytes with SHA-256'

No evidence in codebase that a collision between the pipe-delimited encoding and the new encoding is possible (the plan already separates them by the `prerequisite_id` presence check); the length-prefix framing is additional complexity with no stated benefit over a domain-separated string
findings.finding: The 'Binding signal-chain and identity tests' section specifies a bespoke four-field length-prefix binary encoding (4-byte big-endian uint per field, UTF-8, SHA-256) for `mint_finding_id` when `prerequisite_id` is present. The existing `mint_finding_id` already achieves stable deduplication via `hashlib.sha256(basis.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()` with a pipe-delimited basis string. No rationale is given for why the existing string-based encoding cannot be extended with a domain-separated `prerequisite_id` field (e.g. `'prerequisite-finding-id-v1|' + finding + '|' + criteria + '|' + prerequisite_id`). The bespoke binary protocol (prefixed-length framing) introduces novel serialization code not found elsewhere in the codebase for a single use-case, creating an NIH-style re-invention of the collision-resistance already provided by the existing hash encoding.
findings.location: Binding signal-chain and identity tests section; Implementation section 'Extend `mint_finding_id` to use the authoritative structured identity encoding'
scenarios: Implementor must write and test custom struct-pack serialization code that has no other call-site or prior pattern in this codebase
Future maintainers must understand two distinct binary-vs-string encoding paths for what is semantically the same ID function
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the length-prefix binary protocol with a domain-separated string: `basis = 'prerequisite-finding-id-v1|' + finding_text + '|' + ','.join(sorted(criteria)) + '|' + prerequisite_id`, then `hashlib.sha256(basis.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()[:16]`. This preserves the same collision-separation guarantee (same text from different prerequisite IDs hashes differently) without introducing novel binary serialization code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new prerequisite baseline file requires a distinct schema from the existing `fidelity_spot_eval_results.json` artifact, or whether extending that file with a `prerequisite_packing_singleton` key (matching the existing `s2_relocation`/`s5_packing` structure) could satisfy the baseline-pinning and corpus-digest requirements without introducing a second committed artifact schema.
evidence: Existing `fidelity_spot_eval_results.json` shape (lines 1-74): `{s2_relocation: {passed, gating_failures, metrics: {...}}, s5_packing: {...}}` — no `corpus_digest`, `recorded_at`, or `model` at the top level

Plan specifies new keys: `recall`, `false_accept`, `coverage_completeness`, `prerequisite_attribution_error_rate`, `model`, `corpus_digest`, `recorded_at` — structurally different from the existing results format
`corpus_digest` is a new concept not present in any other eval-spec artifact in the codebase
findings.finding: The 'Binding comparative baseline and compatibility behavior' section introduces a new committed baseline JSON file (`src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json`) with a schema containing `recall`, `false_accept`, `coverage_completeness`, `prerequisite_attribution_error_rate`, `model`, `corpus_digest`, and `recorded_at`. The existing `fidelity_spot_eval_results.json` stores spot-eval results with a different schema (`passed`, `gating_failures`, `metrics`). The new baseline adds `corpus_digest` (for cross-run corpus validation) and `recorded_at` (timestamp) as new structural concepts with no parallel in the existing eval-specs artifact files. This creates a new config-surface artifact schema for a single new eval target rather than extending the existing committed-results pattern.
findings.location: Binding comparative baseline and compatibility behavior section; 'The first-slice command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline`'
scenarios: Two distinct baseline/results file schemas must be understood and maintained separately for the same evaluation infrastructure
If the `corpus_digest` validation concept proves useful it will need to be retrofitted to the existing `fidelity_spot_eval_results.json` format

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or reference an existing test) that proves `verification_model()` and `code_review_verification_model()` produce an identical schema before and after the `_build_verification_output` signature change.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py:180: return _build_verification_output(forbid, SeverityAttrs)  — verification_model
src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py:350: return _build_verification_output(forbid, CodeSeverityAttrs)  — code_review_verification_model
Both share the same _build_verification_output function; any parameter change to it propagates to both call sites
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2' and that `verification_model` and `code_review_verification_model` 'pass no extra field and remain schema-identical'. But both these models call `_build_verification_output` and will be affected by its signature change. The plan simultaneously says to modify `_build_verification_output` AND that the two sibling models 'remain schema-identical' — this is a codebase assertion about non-impact that requires verification but is unverifiable without the implementation, since the function is shared.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'verification_model and code_review_verification_model pass no extra field and remain schema-identical. Do not add the field to shared _build_binary, _BINARY_NA_DEFAULT, or GRADED_BINARY'
scenarios: If the extra-binary-fields default is None and the implementation correctly gates on it, both existing models remain schema-identical — but only if the implementation is done correctly, which the plan does not prove.
A non-defaulted extra parameter would break both existing callers at import time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale explaining why the single `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload is shared between material-pin creation and prerequisite routing (e.g., avoidance of duplicate relation reads, guaranteed consistency between the two consumers seeing the same snapshot, or alignment with the 75de interface contract).
evidence: 'This story neither recollects relations nor changes parent and child context handling' — describes a constraint but not a rationale
AC: 'One relation preload supplies both material-pin creation and prerequisite routing' — stated as a requirement, not justified
Absence of any section explaining why the unified preload is preferred over separate preloads
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why the single `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload from ticket `75de-02b0-d454-410b` is used to serve both material-pin creation and prerequisite routing, rather than treating these as separate concerns with separate preloads or separate reads. The plan asserts 'exactly one relation preload supplies both pin creation and prerequisite routing' as a constraint (AC: 'One relation preload supplies both material-pin creation and prerequisite routing') but does not state why this is the right design — it only describes what it does. This is architecturally significant: if the two consumers have divergent future needs (e.g., pin creation needs all relation roles, prerequisite routing needs only `depends_on`/`blocks`), the shared preload creates coupling that may require coordinated changes.
findings.location: Upstream contract section and AC: 'One relation preload supplies both material-pin creation and prerequisite routing'
scenarios: A future story needs prerequisite routing to include transitive dependencies while pin creation remains direct-only — the shared preload cannot serve both without modification.

An implementer adds a separate prerequisite read thinking it is fine (since the plan never explains why sharing is important), introducing dual reads that violate the isolation contract tested by `test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the bespoke 4-byte big-endian length-prefix binary encoding for prerequisite finding IDs vs established alternatives (UUID v5 namespace hashing, delimiter-separated SHA-256 input, or HMAC-keyed IDs) in the Binding signal-chain section, or adopt a standard scheme.
evidence: 'encode four fields as UTF-8, prefix each with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length, and concatenate them in this order: fixed version string `prerequisite-finding-id-v1`, finding text, comma-joined sorted criteria, and authoritative prerequisite ID. Hash the concatenated bytes with SHA-256'
No mention of UUID v5, HMAC-based IDs, or any existing content-addressed ID standard
findings.finding: The bespoke binary identity encoding (4-byte big-endian length-prefix + UTF-8 concatenation + SHA-256) is a novel micro-protocol with no acknowledgment of standard alternatives such as UUID v5 (RFC 4122 namespace hashing), delimiter-separated structured strings, or HMAC-keyed IDs. The plan asserts this specific wire format without justifying why it is preferable to established content-addressed ID schemes.
findings.location: Binding signal-chain and identity tests — identity encoding definition
scenarios: If the encoding is ever re-implemented (e.g., in a different language or service), the bespoke length-prefix framing introduces interoperability risk that a standard scheme would avoid.
If separator-based alternatives were considered and rejected, the reasoning is unavailable to reviewers, making the choice opaque.
findings.suggested_fix: UUID v5 with a fixed namespace UUID and a structured string `prerequisite-finding-id-v1:{finding_text}:{sorted_criteria}:{prerequisite_id}` is a drop-in alternative that is RFC-standardized, collision-resistant, and trivially reproducible across languages without a custom length-prefix parser.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `models_at_or_above()` exists and cite its module path (e.g., `from rebar.llm.sizing import models_at_or_above`), or define its expected signature and location in the plan.
evidence: 'Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Largest-window exhaustion emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error`'
'escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`' (second occurrence in focused verifier section)
No definition, import path, or module citation provided for this function
findings.finding: `models_at_or_above()` is referenced in both the finder and verifier context-limit escalation paths as an existing callable, but the plan never defines it, imports it, or cites an existing module where it lives. Its existence and contract (returns models ordered by context window for a given model) is an unverified capability assertion.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`'; Binding focused prompt and failure contracts — 'Focused verifier context-limit recovery … escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`'
scenarios: If `models_at_or_above()` does not exist or returns models in the wrong order, the entire context-limit escalation path silently fails or raises an AttributeError at runtime.
If it exists but has a different signature (e.g., requires a provider argument), the plan's usage is incorrect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a docstring or inline comment to the `extra-binary-fields` parameter of `_build_verification_output` (or the `plan_review_verification_model` call site) stating that `prerequisite_attribution_valid` is a plan-review-only veto field — 'no' or 'na' triggers attribution-invalid in `plan_review_decide`'s focused domain, excluded from GRADED_BINARY.
evidence: Plan text: 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with default `na` and vocabulary `yes|no|na`'
Plan text: 'its explicit `na` default and exclusion from `GRADED_BINARY` make it a plan-review-only veto rather than a validity contributor'

Plan text (decide section): 'missing, duplicate, malformed, out-of-range, `no`, or `na` attribution converts that prerequisite record to `indeterminate/attribution-invalid` and removes its incompatibility findings'

Existing `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` and `GRADED_BINARY` patterns in verify_models.py show that veto semantics for `cited_reference_accurate` are co-located with the field definitions — but this new field's veto rule is only in the decide section.
findings.finding: The `prerequisite_attribution_valid` field's veto processing rule ('no' or 'na' converts the prerequisite record to indeterminate/attribution-invalid) is defined in the `plan_review_decide` prose section, but the field's schema definition — added via an 'optional extra-binary-fields argument' to `_build_verification_output` in `passes.py` — carries no co-located decision rule. The vocabulary 'yes|no|na' is defined at the schema level, but an executor reading only the schema section cannot know that 'no' is a veto (not a validity contributor) without reading the decide section.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output`...' and 'In `plan_review_decide`, independently reshape and decide each domain. For the focused domain, missing, duplicate, malformed, out-of-range, `no`, or `na` attribution converts...'
scenarios: An implementer adding the `extra-binary-fields` mechanism to `_build_verification_output` may not know the field has veto (not graded) semantics and might inadvertently add it to GRADED_BINARY or give it 'insufficient' as default.
A test author writing coverage for `_build_verification_output` will not know to test the veto conversion path without reading the decide section.

findings.finding: The Pass-1 focused finder prompt content, structure, and decision criteria are never specified. The plan names `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` (a prompt library ID constant) but never defines the prompt file deliverable, its system-prompt instructions, how prerequisite blocks are formatted in the prompt, or what reasoning the LLM should apply to reach `consistent|finding|indeterminate` dispositions. The existing pattern resolves prompt IDs to `.md` files, but no such file appears as a plan deliverable.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with the prerequisite-only finder route. It receives the subject plan plus stable whole prerequisite blocks and invokes plan_review_prerequisite_coverage.'

findings.finding: The plan explicitly specifies context-limit recovery (recursive bin splitting → model escalation → indeterminate/evaluation-error) for the focused finder path, but does not specify how a context-limit error on the focused verifier call is handled. The verifier receives the subject plan plus finding blocks from the preload and packs 'multiple whole focused records within budget', but if a verifier call exceeds context, the plan is silent on whether to split findings, escalate the model, or map affected findings to attribution-invalid.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Size the actual focused prompt topology ... through a prerequisite-specific adapter in sizing.py' and 'The focused verifier receives the subject plan and, for each local finding, its authoritative prerequisite ID and plan block from the preload ... Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a paragraph under the verifier sizing section: 'On context-limit error during a focused verifier call, split the finding list for that call in half and retry each half independently. Escalate a singleton verifier call (single finding) through models_at_or_above(). If the largest window is exhausted, convert the affected prerequisite record to indeterminate/evaluation-error; do not emit a partial finding result.'

findings.finding: The `Finding` type referenced inside `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord.findings: list[Finding]` is never defined. The plan names the outer wrapper `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput { records: list[PrerequisiteCoverageRecord] }` and states each record has `findings: list[Finding]`, but `Finding` is not identified as the generic plan-review finding dict, a new local dataclass, or a reference to an existing type. An implementer cannot know what fields this list element must carry (text, criteria, prerequisite_id, etc.) without a definition.
findings.location: Implementation section: '_prerequisite_coverage_model() in passes.py with outer PrerequisiteCoverageOutput { records: list[PrerequisiteCoverageRecord] }. Each record is {prerequisite_id: canonical string, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding], …}'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'Each `Finding` in `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord.findings` is a standard plan-review finding dict (`finding`, `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`) augmented with the mandatory `prerequisite_id` field (the same canonical string as the parent record's `prerequisite_id`).' This makes the AC 'every upheld focused finding appears in the verdict with its authoritative prerequisite_id' mechanically verifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback / back-out plan or expand-contract sequence for the `finalize_verdict` modification — e.g., a feature flag (`prerequisite_indeterminate_enabled`) that defaults to false until the prerequisite system is proven stable, allowing safe revert without a full code deploy.
evidence: 'immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`, with existing branches changed to `elif`'
'The unchanged signing boundary signs only PASS'
No feature flag, rollback path, or expand-contract sequence described for this behavioral change to finalize_verdict
findings.finding: The explicit modification to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` (inserting the `prerequisite_indeterminate` branch before `if blocking`) changes verdict behavior for all callers of `finalize_verdict` that pass a coverage dict. If a bug in the new prerequisite evaluation system causes spurious `indeterminate` records, all reviews will be forced to INDETERMINATE with no rollback path, feature flag, or back-out mechanism specified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`' and Binding comparative baseline section.
scenarios: A bug in prerequisite preload parsing or schema validation causes every review to receive indeterminate coverage, forcing all verdicts to INDETERMINATE — with no way to disable the new branch without a code deploy rollback.
A provider outage causes mass `evaluation-error` records, blocking all reviews; no circuit-breaker or graceful-degradation path is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate_enabled', False)` guard or a YAML-wired toggle so the new INDETERMINATE branch can be disabled at runtime. Document the rollback step: 'set flag to false to restore prior verdict behavior while the prerequisite system is diagnosed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Establish and consistently use one term for the prerequisite ticket identifier throughout the plan (e.g., 'authoritative canonical ID'), and add a one-line definition at first use in the Implementation section (e.g., 'the authoritative canonical ticket ID as supplied by the material-pin interface').
evidence: Implementation: 'Each record is {prerequisite_id: canonical string, ...}'
Binding signal-chain: 'encode four fields... authoritative prerequisite ID'
AC: 'Every upheld focused finding appears in the verdict with its authoritative prerequisite ID'
Binding focused prompt: 'The prompt must return PrerequisiteCoverageOutput with exactly one record for every supplied canonical ID'
Binding implementation sequence step 1: 'any caught finding has `pred_prerequisite_id != gold_prerequisite_id`'
findings.finding: The term denoting a prerequisite's ticket identifier is used inconsistently across sections: 'authoritative canonical ticket ID', 'canonical string', 'canonical ID', 'authoritative ID', 'canonical ticket ID', and 'authoritative canonical ID' all appear, apparently referring to the same concept. While context usually disambiguates, the schema field is named `prerequisite_id` with type `canonical string`, and the identity encoding section refers to 'authoritative prerequisite ID', making it ambiguous whether 'authoritative' is an attribute of the ID type or a synonym for 'canonical'.
findings.location: Throughout: Implementation ('canonical string', 'canonical ID'), Binding signal-chain ('authoritative prerequisite ID'), Binding focused prompt ('authoritative canonical ticket ID', 'canonical IDs'), AC lines ('authoritative prerequisite ID', 'canonical `prerequisite_id`')
scenarios: Implementer uses a non-authoritative form of the ID in the identity encoding, producing different SHA-256 hashes than a colleague who uses the authoritative form, causing finding ID mismatches across environments.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick 'authoritative canonical ID' as the single term; do a search-replace pass in the plan to normalize all variants; add a parenthetical definition at first use in Implementation: '(the stable canonical ticket ID string supplied by the `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the maximum number of direct prerequisites the focused path will accept per review (e.g., a hard cap N_MAX), and specify the behavior (skip, warn, or truncate) when that cap is exceeded, to bound per-review LLM call count and cost.
evidence: Plan defines `pack_prerequisite_bins` with greedy packing but no stated maximum prerequisite count or maximum bin count.

'Order blocks lexicographically by canonical ID. Compute limit = int(largest_window_tokens(model) * headroom). Greedily append while fixed plus the sum of block charges is at most limit' — the outer loop is unbounded by prerequisite count.
No AC or metric caps the number of direct prerequisites accepted as input to the focused path.
The gold corpus uses sets of 'at least three prerequisites' and 'one multi-bin' but states no production ceiling.
findings.finding: The plan introduces per-review LLM call fan-out proportional to the number of direct prerequisites (finder calls + verifier calls), but specifies no upper bound on the number of prerequisites a ticket may have. Because `pack_prerequisite_bins` packs blocks into bins but does not cap the total number of bins, a ticket with many prerequisites can generate an unbounded number of focused LLM calls and therefore unbounded per-review cost with no stated economic ceiling or safety valve.
findings.location: Implementation — `pack_prerequisite_bins` definition; Binding implementation sequence step 4
scenarios: A ticket with 50 direct prerequisites produces 50 prerequisite blocks; packing may yield many bins each requiring a separate finder call, followed by a verifier call per finding — multiplying the per-review LLM cost by an order of magnitude with no cap.
A pathological ticket (e.g., a mega-refactor that depends on dozens of stories) triggers dozens of focused calls in a single review, exhausting token budgets or incurring unexpected API cost.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or implementation note: 'Cap focused prerequisite evaluation at N_MAX=20 direct prerequisites per review. When the preload contains more than N_MAX readable prerequisites, evaluate the first N_MAX in stable lexicographic order and emit a single `indeterminate/evaluation-error` record with `detail=prerequisite-count-exceeded` for the remainder.' Adjust `pack_prerequisite_bins` to reject input exceeding this cap before bin layout.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` to the file-impact list, since `_build_verification_output` requires a signature change and the two existing call sites must be verified to remain correct after the change.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py:108: def _build_verification_output(forbid: Any, severity_cls: type) -> type:
Plan: 'Extend _build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields argument'
No third parameter exists; verification_model and code_review_verification_model both call _build_verification_output(forbid, SeverityAttrs) with two arguments
findings.finding: The plan instructs: 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with default `na`'. The actual function signature in `verify_models.py` is `_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls) -> type` — it accepts exactly two parameters. Adding an extra-binary-fields argument is new work, not an extension of an existing optional parameter.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend _build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only plan_review_verification_model pass prerequisite_attribution_valid'
scenarios: Implementer reads 'extend with an optional argument' as if a hook already exists; the function signature change also risks breaking the two call sites (verification_model, code_review_verification_model) if the extra-fields argument is not carefully defaulted.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan to: 'Add an optional `extra_binary_fields: dict | None = None` parameter to `_build_verification_output`; when supplied, those fields are appended to `binary_fields` before `create_model`. Existing callers (`verification_model`, `code_review_verification_model`) pass no extra fields and are unchanged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/production_batch_runner.py` to the file-impact list and explicitly describe how the prerequisite-only finder route integrates with (or is separated from) the existing `ProductionBatchRunner.run()` path.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/production_batch_runner.py: ProductionBatchRunner.run() calls assemble_context + run_pass1 — no prerequisite routing
Plan does not list production_batch_runner.py in any file-impact section
PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER / PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER absent from passes.py (confirmed grep)
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Add `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` and `PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER = "plan-review-prerequisite-verifier"` in `passes.py`' and 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with the prerequisite-only finder route.' The existing `ProductionBatchRunner.run()` in `production_batch_runner.py` has no prerequisite routing path. The plan treats the extension as trivial, but the `ProductionBatchRunner` reconstructs a full `PlanContext` via `assemble_context()` and routes findings through `run_pass1()`. A prerequisite-only route is structurally different (it doesn't call `run_pass1`). This is new code, not an extension of an existing seam — the file `production_batch_runner.py` is absent from the plan's file-impact section.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the production Pass-1 batch runner with the prerequisite-only finder route.'
scenarios: Implementer adds PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER to passes.py but has no clear seam in ProductionBatchRunner for the prerequisite-only route — the runner reconstructs PlanContext for general criteria, but the prerequisite route needs a PlanRelationSnapshot input, not a PlanContext.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `fidelity_spot_eval.py` currently has no CLI entrypoint — the `--prerequisite-packing`, `--singleton`, and `--baseline` flags require adding a new `if __name__ == '__main__'` (or `__main__.py`) entry from scratch.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/fidelity_spot_eval.py: no 'if __name__' block found
__all__ at line 280: ['RELOCATED_PROMPTS', 'relocation_spot_eval', 'packing_spot_eval', 'record_results', 'load_recorded_results', 'recorded_results_path'] — no CLI entrypoint
Verification section invokes: '.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing' — a -m invocation requires __main__ or a module-level entry in the package
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Add separate opt-in `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval` and a module CLI entry point `--prerequisite-packing` in `fidelity_spot_eval.py`' — treating the module CLI entry point as an extension of existing behavior. But `fidelity_spot_eval.py` has no `if __name__ == "__main__"` block and no CLI at all today. Creating a CLI entry point is net-new work.
findings.location: Implementation section (fidelity section): 'Add separate opt-in prerequisite_packing_spot_eval and a module CLI entry point --prerequisite-packing in fidelity_spot_eval.py'
scenarios: The Binding comparative baseline section commands (--singleton --write-baseline, --baseline) also require a CLI that does not yet exist. If the implementer treats this as 'adding to existing CLI' they may be surprised it does not exist and under-scope the work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` to the plan's file-impact list, explicitly noting that `_build_verification_output` gains an optional `extra-binary-fields` argument consumed only by `plan_review_verification_model`.
evidence: The plan states: 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid`'
`_build_verification_output` lives at `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` lines 108–136 (confirmed by search)
The plan's Implementation section names files to touch but never mentions `verify_models.py` or the `review_kernel` package as edit targets
This file is outside the `plan_review/` directory — it is a cross-directory consumer that needs updating
findings.finding: `_build_verification_output` in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` is implicitly an edit target (the plan says to extend it with an optional `extra-binary-fields` argument so `plan_review_verification_model` can pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid`), but `verify_models.py` is never named as an edit target in any file-impact list or implementation section of the plan.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument'
scenarios: A developer implementing this plan works through each named file but skips `verify_models.py` because it is never named, resulting in `plan_review_verification_model` being unable to pass the new attribution field.
A reviewer checking file impact misses the kernel-side change, which affects any future code-review or kernel verification that shares `_build_verification_output`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit file-impact bullet: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py — extend _build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields parameter; only plan_review_verification_model passes prerequisite_attribution_valid; verification_model and code_review_verification_model are unchanged.`
